1: wîhôšuaʿ zāqēn bāʾ bayyāmîm — 'Joshua was old, advanced in days.' The idiom bāʾ bayyāmîm ('come into days') appears elsewhere only of Abraham (Gen 24:1) and David (1 Kgs 1:1); it signals a leader at the end of his productive season. YHWH's immediate response — 'much land remains to be possessed' — frames the problem: the conquest is unfinished, and the leader is aging. The tension between incomplete possession and approaching death drives chapters 13-24.
2: zōʾt hāʾāreṣ hanniśʾāret — 'this is the land that remains.' The admission that land remains contradicts the conquest summaries of 11:23 and 21:43 only if those summaries are taken as absolute rather than judicial. The ideal ('all the land was taken') and the realistic ('land remains') coexist — the former is a theological verdict on YHWH's faithfulness; the latter is a historical acknowledgment of incomplete execution.
3: The Philistine pentapolis: Gaza, Ashdod, Ashkelon, Gath, Ekron. hammiṣraʾ ʾašer ʿal-pĕnê miṣrayim — 'the Shihor, which is before Egypt' — marks the southwestern boundary. The Philistine cities are counted as Canaanite territory (liknāʿanî teḥāšēb) — a legal categorization that will later matter for how their continued occupation is understood.
4: Southward: Canaanite territory and Mearah of the Sidonians. The inclusion of Sidonian territory (Phoenician coast) within the land's ideal boundaries reflects the Gen 15:18-21 promise ('from the river of Egypt to the great river, the Euphrates'). The actual possession never reached these limits; the promise remains partially unfulfilled, pointing forward to the ultimate Davidic/Solomonic expansion (1 Kgs 4:21) and beyond.
5: Lebanon from Baal-gad to Lebo-hamath — the northern boundaries of the ideal land. The Giblites (haggĕblî) are the inhabitants of Byblos (Gebal), a major Phoenician port. These northern territories remain unoccupied throughout the monarchy period; their inclusion in the 'remaining land' acknowledges the gap between promise and possession.
6: ʾănî ʾōrîšennem mippĕnê bĕnê yiśrāʾēl — 'I will drive them out before Israel.' YHWH's first-person commitment — 'I will do it' — shifts responsibility back to divine action. Joshua's role is now to divide the land by lot (raq happilah lĕyiśrāʾēl bĕnaḥălāh); the driving out will happen as Israel obeys within its allotments. The promise structure: YHWH acts as Israel advances.
7: wĕʿattāh ḥalleq ʾet-hāʾāreṣ hazzōʾt bĕnaḥălāh — 'now divide this land as inheritance.' The imperative and the noun naḥălāh (inheritance) together signal a major structural transition: from conquest narrative to inheritance distribution. Naḥălāh (appearing ~200× in the OT) denotes permanent familial patrimony — land held not by right of conquest alone but by covenantal grant, passed through generations.
8: Review of the trans-Jordan inheritance given by Moses. The Reubenites, Gadites, and half-tribe of Manasseh received their allotments east of the Jordan (Num 32); this chapter confirms those grants. The Mosaic inheritance is the precedent and model for the Josuaic distribution now beginning.
9: Trans-Jordan geography: Aroer by the Arnon, the city in the middle of the valley, the Medeba plateau. The mishor ('plateau/tableland') is the characteristic terrain of Moab and trans-Jordan Reuben — flat, elevated plain east of the Dead Sea. The geographic details are the property records of Reubenite land tenure.
10: All the cities of Sihon up to the Ammonite border. ʿad-gĕbûl bĕnê ʿammôn — 'to the border of the Ammonites.' The Ammonites are intentionally excluded; Deut 2:19 forbids Israel from taking Ammonite territory ('I have given it to the sons of Lot as a possession'). These land grants respect the existing distribution of peoples even as they claim Amorite territory.
11: Gilead, the territory of Geshurites and Maacathites, and all Mount Hermon. wĕhakkol haggĕšûrî wĕhammaakatî — the Geshurites and Maacathites are included in the allotment description but not in the actual possession, as v.13 will immediately note. The legal grant and the actual possession diverge; the gap is acknowledged but not resolved.
12: kol-mamleket ʿōg babbāšān ʾašer-mālaḵ bĕʿaštārôt ûbĕʾedreʿî hûʾ nišʾar miyeter hārĕpāʾîm — 'the whole kingdom of Og in Bashan, who reigned at Ashtaroth and Edrei; he alone remained of the remnant of the Rephaim.' The Rephaim identification is repeated from ch.12:4, maintaining the motif: this territory was won from the last of a dying race of giant peoples. The conquest of trans-Jordan was a conquest of the uncanny as much as the military.
13: wĕlōʾ hōrîšû bĕnê yiśrāʾēl ʾet-haggĕšûrî wĕʾet-hammaʿăkātî — 'the Israelites did not drive out the Geshurites or the Maacathites.' The first of the failure notices that punctuate the allotment chapters. wayyēšeb haggĕšûrî wĕhammaakatî bĕqereb yiśrāʾēl ʿad hayyôm hazzeh — 'they live among Israel to this day.' The 'to this day' formula marks the note as etiological — explaining a visible present reality by reference to a past decision. Geshur's survival will eventually produce Maacah, who becomes David's wife and Absalom's mother (2 Sam 3:3; 13:37) — an unconquered people becomes a dynastic entanglement.
14: raq lĕšēbeṭ halēwî lōʾ-nātan naḥălāh — 'only to the tribe of Levi he did not give an inheritance.' The raq ('only/except') marks Levi as the single anomaly in the distribution system. ʾiššê YHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl hûʾ naḥălātô — 'the fire-offerings of YHWH God of Israel are their inheritance.' The naḥălāh of YHWH's fire-offerings (ʾiššê) replaces territory; Levi's sustenance comes from the altar, not the land. This principle — that priestly service to YHWH constitutes an inheritance — is the theological foundation for the NT principle that ministers of the gospel are supported by those they serve (1 Cor 9:13-14).
15: The Reubenite allotment begins. wayyittēn mōšeh lĕmaṭṭēh bĕnê rĕʾûbēn lĕmišpĕḥōtām — 'Moses gave to the tribe of Reuben according to their clans.' The distribution unit is the mišpāḥāh (clan/extended family group), not the individual; the inheritance is corporate and generational.
16: Reuben's territory: Aroer by the Arnon, the city in the middle of the valley, and the Medeba plateau. The precise boundary descriptions use the landmark-and-feature format of ANE boundary texts (parallel to Egyptian and Hittite treaty boundary clauses). Each named landmark is a legally recognized point in the property description.
17: Cities within Reuben's allotment: Heshbon (Sihon's capital), Dibon, Bamoth-baal, Beth-baal-meon. The presence of baal-names in the allotted territory (Beth-baal-meon, Bamoth-baal) reflects Canaanite place-names that Israel inherited without immediately renaming — the toponym preserves the religious history of the land even as it changes ownership.
18: Jahaz, Kedemoth, Mephaath — sites along Reuben's eastern border. Jahaz was where Sihon attacked Israel (Num 21:23; Deut 2:32), making its inclusion in Reuben's allotment a direct conversion of battlefield to farmland.
19: Kiriathaim, Sibmah, Zereth-shahar on the hill in the valley — Reuben's hill country. Sibmah was known for its vineyards (Isa 16:8-9; Jer 48:32) — the allotment assigns productive agricultural land, not merely boundary markers.
20: Beth-peor, the slopes of Pisgah, Beth-jeshimoth. Beth-peor is freighted with memory: it was at Peor that Israel committed apostasy with Moabite women (Num 25:3; Deut 4:3). Moses was buried 'in the valley opposite Beth-peor' (Deut 34:6). Reuben's inheritance includes sites of Israel's deepest failures — the land is taken in full, shame included.
21: All the cities of the plateau (ʿārê hammiššôr) and the kingdom of Sihon. The formula kol-mamleket sîḥōn melek hāʾĕmōrî ʾašer mālaḵ bĕḥešbôn — 'the whole kingdom of Sihon king of the Amorites who reigned at Heshbon' — signals that Reuben inherits not just territory but sovereignty: what was a Canaanite kingdom becomes an Israelite tribal holding.
22: wĕʾet-bilʿām ben-bĕʿôr haqqōsēm hārĕgû bĕnê yiśrāʾēl beḥereb — 'Balaam son of Beor the diviner, the Israelites killed with the sword.' The term qōsēm (diviner, one who practices qĕsāmôt) labels Balaam by his means of knowledge, not just his role. In Num 22-24 Balaam functions as a prophet (nābîʾ-like figure) who speaks YHWH's words; here he is demoted to qōsēm — one whose methods are prohibited in Israel (Deut 18:10). The retroactive reclassification reflects his ultimate fate: a man who blessed Israel was killed as a collaborator in Midian's seduction of Israel (Num 31:8,16). The NT references to Balaam (2 Pet 2:15; Jude 11; Rev 2:14) follow this pejorative labeling.
23: The Jordan and its border constitute Reuben's western boundary. gĕbûl bĕnê rĕʾûbēn wayyihyeh hayyardēn ûgĕbûl — 'the Jordan and its bank was their border.' The Jordan is both boundary and identity marker: the trans-Jordan tribes are defined by their position east of the river, which will become a point of tension in ch.22 (the altar dispute).
24: The Gadite allotment begins. wayyittēn mōšeh lĕmaṭṭēh gād lĕmišpĕḥōtām — structural parallel to the Reubenite grant formula.
25: Gad's territory: Jazer, all the cities of Gilead, and half the Ammonite territory 'to Aroer which is before Rabbah.' The Ammonite adjacency makes Gad the eastern frontier tribe, bordering the perennial Ammonite opponents. Gilead's association with Gad is so strong that 'Gilead' becomes a shorthand for the trans-Jordan tribes (Judg 5:17; 10:18).
26: From Heshbon to Ramath-mizpeh and Betonim; from Mahanaim to the border of Debir. Mahanaim is Jacob's site of angelic encounter (Gen 32:2), where he named the place 'two camps'; it becomes an Israelite administrative and military base (2 Sam 2:8; 17:24; 1 Kgs 2:8). Jacob's patriarchal sites become Gadite cities.
27: In the valley: Beth-haram, Beth-nimrah, Succoth, Zaphon — these are the Jordan valley floor settlements, fertile lowlands east of the river. Succoth was Jacob's first settlement returning from Laban (Gen 33:17), another patriarchal site incorporated into the tribal map.
28: The Gadite inheritance concludes: cities and their villages. The total is measured against the Mosaic grant formula: zōʾt naḥălat bĕnê-gād lĕmišpĕḥōtām — 'this is the inheritance of the Gadites by their clans.' The clan-unit distribution ensures generational continuity.
29: Half-Manasseh's trans-Jordan territory. wayyittēn mōšeh laḥăṣî šēbet hamnașṣeh — Moses distributes to the half-tribe, reflecting the unusual division of Manasseh between trans-Jordan (half) and Cisjordan (half). This split-tribe arrangement creates structural complexity in the later allotment chapters (Josh 17) as both halves are allocated separately.
30: Half-Manasseh's territory from Mahanaim through all Bashan: kol-baššān kol-mamleket ʿōg babbāšān wĕkol-ḥawwōt yāʾîr — 'all Bashan, the whole kingdom of Og, and all the tent-villages of Jair.' The Havvoth-jair ('tent-villages of Jair') represent a form of semi-nomadic settlement, named for the Manassite hero Jair (Num 32:41) — personal-name places that encode the history of conquest in the landscape.
31: Half of Gilead and Og's cities (Ashtaroth and Edrei) go to half-Manasseh. lĕbnê mākhîr ben-manašše laḥăṣî — 'to the descendants of Machir son of Manasseh, for half of them.' Machir is the dominant Manassite clan; the allotment recognizes clan-level distinctions within the tribe.
32: ʾēlleh ʾašer niḥal mōšeh bĕʿarābōt môʾāb — 'these are what Moses distributed in the plains of Moab.' The trans-Jordan distribution is retrospective: Eleazar and Joshua had not yet begun; the Cisjordan allotments are still ahead. The chapter closes the Mosaic distribution before opening Joshua's.
33: wĕlišēbeṭ halēwî lōʾ-nātan mōšeh naḥălāh — 'to the tribe of Levi Moses gave no inheritance.' The Levitical exception closes the chapter and mirrors v.14. YHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl hûʾ naḥălātām — 'YHWH God of Israel is their inheritance.' The hûʾ ('he is') is emphatic — not just any inheritance but YHWH himself as the inalienable possession. This theological principle is picked up in Ps 16:5 ('YHWH is my chosen portion and my cup') and pervades the NT understanding of God as the believer's ultimate inheritance (Eph 1:14,18; 1 Pet 1:4).
